target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #45508: [Feature]: Self-hosted STT/TTS provider support in webchat (Route webchat TTS through the gateway instead of browser Speech API)

- Title: [Feature]: Self-hosted STT/TTS provider support in webchat (Route webchat TTS through the gateway instead of browser Speech API)
- State: OPEN
- Author: mcfex

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

The webchat "Read aloud" button and "Voice input" mic both use the browser's built-in Web Speech APIs (SpeechSynthesis and Web Speech API). This completely ignores any TTS/STT configuration in openclaw.json, making self-hosted voice setups invisible to the webchat frontend.

### Problem to solve

Users who have configured messages.tts with a self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoint (e.g. Speaches/Kokoro) get no benefit from that setup in webchat. The "Read aloud" button calls window.speechSynthesis directly, bypassing the gateway entirely and using whatever OS voice is active — often the wrong language and voice. Similarly, the mic button sends audio to Google's Web Speech API regardless of whether a local Whisper-compatible STT endpoint is available.

### Proposed solution

- When messages.tts is configured, the "Read aloud" button should POST to the gateway's TTS endpoint and play back the returned audio blob, instead of using window.speechSynthesis

- Introduce a messages.stt config block (mirroring the existing messages.tts.openai structure) that, when set, routes webchat mic input through the configured STT endpoint (/v1/audio/transcriptions) instead of the browser's Web Speech API

### Alternatives considered

Keeping a separate external voice UI that intercepts messages and handles TTS/STT independently. This works but is fragile, requires maintaining a parallel stack, and duplicates functionality that OpenClaw should own natively.

### Impact

Self-hosted and privacy-conscious users running local TTS/STT (Speaches, Whisper, Kokoro, etc.) currently get zero benefit from their setup in webchat. This change would make webchat a first-class voice interface for anyone running an OpenAI-compatible audio stack — no cloud, no browser STT, full control.

### Evidence/examples

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_
```

```markdown
- GMTekAI at 2026-03-14T05:11:09Z:
I dug through the current tree, and I think this is worth splitting into two scopes because the TTS half is much more implementation-ready than the STT half.

The current Control UI/WebChat bundle in `ui/` really is using browser speech APIs today:
- `ui/src/ui/chat/speech.ts` implements STT with `SpeechRecognition` / `webkitSpeechRecognition` and TTS with `speechSynthesis`
- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts` wires the assistant “Read aloud” button to `speakText(...)`
- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts` wires the mic button to `startStt(...)`

So the core report is valid.

That said, I would be careful about the proposed config/API shape:

1. TTS likely does **not** need a new HTTP endpoint or new config
   Gateway already exposes TTS RPC in `src/gateway/server-methods/tts.ts`, including `tts.convert`, and existing TTS config already lives under `messages.tts`.
   So the smallest TTS fix looks like:
   - when WebChat/Control UI wants playback, call existing `tts.convert`
   - fetch/play the returned audio file/blob
   - only fall back to browser `speechSynthesis` when gateway TTS is unavailable or unsupported

2. STT probably should **not** introduce a parallel `messages.stt` block
   The repo already has a central audio transcription config surface under `tools.media.audio`, and the runtime entry point is `src/media-understanding/transcribe-audio.ts`.
   Introducing `messages.stt` would create a second audio-provider config path next to the one th

[comments/context truncated after 1500 chars]
```

### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

OpenClaw selects a local `openai-completions` proxy as a fallback candidate after the base URL is corrected to `/v1`, but the agent turn still does not complete through that provider even though direct authenticated `POST /v1/chat/completions` requests succeed against the same endpoint and model.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure a local OpenAI-compatible provider with base URL `http://127.0.0.1:8317/v1` and API mode `openai-completions`.
2. Put that provider near the front of the fallback list.
3. Intentionally poison the primary model identifier to force fallback.
4. Trigger a minimal agent turn.
5. Observe that OpenClaw selects the proxy-backed model as a fallback candidate but does not complete through it and falls through to the next provider.
6. Separately verify the same proxy accepts `GET /v1/models` and an authenticated minimal `POST /v1/chat/completions` request against model `z-ai-glm-5`.

### Expected behavior

If a provider in `openai-completions` mode can list models, accept an authenticated minimal `POST /v1/chat/completions`, and is selected as the next fallback candidate, OpenClaw should either complete successfully through it or log the specific request-shape incompatibility clearly enough to diagnose.

### Actual behavior

After the base URL fix, OpenClaw recognizes the provider and selects it as a fallback candidate during real agent execution, but the completion path still does not succeed through that provider and OpenClaw falls through to the next fallback without enough detail to identify which request feature is incompatible.

### OpenClaw version

2026.4.11

### Operating system

Ubuntu Linux

### Install method

npm global

### Model

Primary intentionally invalid to force fallback; fallback model verified directly: `z-ai-glm-5`

### Provider / routing chain

openclaw -> local OpenAI-compatible proxy (`http://127.0.0.1:8317/v1`) -> upstream model/provider

### Additional provider/model setup details

Provider API mode is `openai-completions`. Before the `/v1` path correction, the provider could be skipped due to path failure. After correcting the base URL, OpenClaw now considers/selects it during fallback. Direct verified proxy behavior: `GET /v1/models` succeeds; authenticated minimal `POST /v1/chat/completions` succeeds with body:

```json
{
  "model": "z-ai-glm-5",
  "messages": [

      "role": "user",
      "content": "Re
```

Body:
```markdown
### Summary
This is an integration-hardening / defense-in-depth issue, not a claim that OpenClaw is the sole root cause of Gemini auth drift.

We just finished a local incident/debugging cycle where a risky Gemini ACP path could drift durable Gemini auth away from `oauth-personal` and into the API-key lane when `GEMINI_API_KEY` was ambient.

The deepest bug seam appears to belong upstream in Gemini CLI, because ACP auth handling seems able to persist a fallback auth method back into durable Gemini settings.

However, OpenClaw also contributed to the exposure: the ACP launch/runtime path allowed provider auth env to be inherited broadly. That is a defensible default in some environments, but in a mixed-credential host it makes the wrong auth lane eligible unless the integration hardens against it explicitly.

### What local stage validation showed
We ran an isolated-HOME comparison without touching live user state.

#### Old risky path
- no explicit outer auth selector for the Gemini ACP lane
- bare Gemini ACP launch path
- ambient `GEMINI_API_KEY`

Result:
- the staged auth state drifted to the API-key lane

#### Hardened local path
- Gemini durable settings pinned to `oauth-personal`
- explicit outer selection guard for the Gemini ACP lane
- Gemini launch path strips Gemini / GenAI auth env before exec
- gateway/runtime no longer carries broad Gemini auth env by default

Result:
- the same staged pressure did not drift the auth state back

### Why file this in OpenClaw
Even if the deepest fix lands upstream in Gemini CLI, OpenClaw still has useful product-level hardening work here.

The lesson is broader than Gemini:

> ACP-backed agent integrations that depend on a specific auth mode should be able to declare that auth contract explicitly, scrub incompatible ambient auth env, and fail closed when the contract is not met.

### Suggested product direction
#### 1. Auth-contract metadata on ACP agent definitions
Allow an ACP-backed agent or route to declare:
- expected auth mode
- whether fallback auth methods are allowed
- whether ambient provider auth env should be stripped

#### 2. Integration-level env scrubbing hooks
Provide a first-class way to remove provider auth env from ACP launches when a route is supposed to use another auth surface.

For example, Gemini ACP routes should be able to scrub things like:
- `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- `GOOGLE_API_KEY`
- `GOOGLE_GENAI_API_KEY`
- `GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_VERTEXAI`
- `GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA`

#### 3. Fail-closed prefligh

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```
